# Copy Editing Checklist

Verify mechanical correctness and internal consistency. This is the final polish pass — structure and prose should already be addressed.

## 1. Grammar & Syntax

- [ ] Subject-verb agreement
- [ ] Correct verb tenses (no unintentional shifts)
- [ ] Pronoun-antecedent agreement and clarity
- [ ] Dangling or misplaced modifiers
- [ ] Run-on sentences and comma splices
- [ ] Sentence fragments (intentional vs. accidental)
- [ ] Parallelism in lists and compound structures
- [ ] Correct use of who/whom, that/which, lay/lie, affect/effect

## 2. Punctuation

- [ ] Commas: serial (Oxford) comma consistency, introductory phrases, compound sentences
- [ ] Em dashes vs. en dashes vs. hyphens — used correctly
- [ ] Semicolons joining independent clauses properly
- [ ] Quotation marks: correct placement with other punctuation
- [ ] Apostrophes: possessives vs. contractions vs. plurals
- [ ] Ellipses: three dots with proper spacing (or four at end of sentence)
- [ ] Exclamation marks: used sparingly — let the prose do the work

## 3. Spelling & Typos

- [ ] Commonly confused words (their/there/they're, its/it's, your/you're)
- [ ] Homophones in context
- [ ] Proper nouns capitalized consistently
- [ ] Technical terms spelled correctly
- [ ] No autocorrect artifacts

## 4. Consistency

### Style Consistency
- [ ] Numbers: spelled out vs. numerals (pick one rule and hold it)
- [ ] Date and time formats consistent
- [ ] Abbreviations introduced before use and consistent throughout
- [ ] Capitalization rules consistent (title case vs. sentence case in headers)
- [ ] British vs. American English consistent (colour/color, -ise/-ize)

### Internal Consistency
- [ ] Character names spelled the same throughout
- [ ] Place names consistent
- [ ] Physical descriptions don't contradict (eye color, height, etc.)
- [ ] Timeline makes sense (days of week, seasons, elapsed time)
- [ ] Distances and geography are plausible
- [ ] Technical details are consistent (weapon types, vehicle models, etc.)
- [ ] Weather and time of day track logically through scenes

## 5. Point of View

- [ ] POV consistent within scenes
- [ ] No head-hopping (seeing thoughts of non-POV characters)
- [ ] If omniscient, transitions between perspectives are smooth
- [ ] POV character doesn't know things they shouldn't know
- [ ] First person: narrator doesn't describe their own expressions they can't see

## 6. Tense

- [ ] Primary tense consistent (past vs. present)
- [ ] Tense shifts for flashbacks are intentional and clearly signaled
- [ ] Verb tenses in dialogue attribution consistent
- [ ] Conditional and subjunctive used correctly

## 7. Formatting

- [ ] Paragraph breaks at appropriate points
- [ ] Scene breaks clearly marked
- [ ] Emphasis (italics/bold) used consistently and sparingly
- [ ] Block quotes or extracts formatted uniformly
- [ ] Headers follow a consistent hierarchy
- [ ] Lists use consistent formatting (punctuation, capitalization)

## 8. Fact-Checking (where applicable)

- [ ] Historical dates and events accurate
- [ ] Geographic details plausible
- [ ] Technical claims verifiable
- [ ] Quotes attributed correctly
- [ ] Links and references functional (for digital content)

## 9. Word Count & Structure

- [ ] Note total word count
- [ ] Note word count per section/chapter
- [ ] Flag significant imbalances (one chapter twice the length of others)
- [ ] Flag repeated words within close proximity (same word in consecutive sentences)
